title: Control Weakness Heatmap — Failed CIS Benchmark Checks

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST CSF ID.RA-02 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF ID.RA-02 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.20: Control Weakness Heatmap — Failed CIS Benchmark Checks.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.20' running on tenable:sc:compliance, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=compliance sourcetype=tenable:sc:compliance earliest=-14d result="Failed"
| stats count as failures by host, check_name
| where failures>3
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities by Vulnerabilities.severity, Vulnerabilities.dest, Vulnerabilities.signature | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Vulnerabilities data model (Vulnerabilities.Vulnerabilities).
description: Surfaces hosts with repeated failed configuration benchmark controls to quantify inherent risk before exploitation.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes compliance and sourcetypes tenable:sc:compliance. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Control Weakness Heatmap — Failed CIS Benchmark Checks. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework tells us what good practice looks like, and we keep evidence we match it.
implementation: (1) Map checks to CSF subcategories; (2) assign remediation owners; (3) track burn-down; (4) document residual risk acceptance. Indexes required: `index=compliance`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=tenable:sc:compliance`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Tenable.sc compliance or OSCAP.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `tenable:sc:compliance`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map checks to CSF subcategories; (2) assign remediation owners; (3) track burn-down; (4) document residual risk acceptance.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=compliance sourcetype=tenable:sc:compliance earliest=-14d result="Failed"
| stats count as failures by host, check_name
| where failures>3
```

Understanding this SPL

**Control Weakness Heatmap — Failed CIS Benchmark Checks (ID.RA-02)** — Surfaces hosts with repeated failed configuration benchmark controls to quantify inherent risk before exploitation.

Documented **Data sources**: `tenable:sc:compliance`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Tenable.sc compliance or OSCAP. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: compliance; **sourcetype**: tenable:sc:compliance. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=compliance, sourcetype=tenable:sc:compliance, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, check_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where failures>3` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Heatmap, Table
knownFalsePositives: Identification gaps during new asset onboarding, M&A integration, or after refresh waves.
